# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queues: carry `(pool id, buffer offset)`
   descriptors over the existing SPSC ring so any message
   travels over any queue; per-process pool registry to
   resolve ids
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
2. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
3. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in

content.text:
Clippy is clean. Here's the review of the uncommitted demo changes.

**What changed:** `Msg` shrank from two words (`seq` + `val`) to one; the old `ring_demo` was renamed `ring_one_msg_2t`; a new single-threaded `ring_one_msg_1t` variant was added and reported first, so the output now separates the ring's own cost from the cross-thread cost — a nice parallel to the existing `pool_demo` / `pool_alloc_free_demo` split.

Findings, roughly in order of importance:

1. **Stale `Msg` doc comment** (`src/bin/zc-ring-x1-demo.rs:33`): "two words so a torn write would show" — the struct is now one word, so the comment is wrong, and the torn-write-detection property it describes is actually gone. If dropping `val` was deliberate (smaller message = cleaner perf number), the doc should say what the one-word message is for instead; if the torn-write canary was still wanted, this is a semantic regression in the 2t test, not just a doc nit.
2. **Copy-paste panic messages in the new 1t function** (lines 84, 93): they say `ring_demo_1t` (a name that never existed — the function is `ring_one_msg_1t`) and "spin_loop SHOULD NOT HAPPEN" though this function has no spin loop. Something like `"ring_one_msg_1t: Full impossible: every produce is followed by a consume"` would say why it can't happen.
3. **Commented-out debug `println!`** at line 80 — leftover scaffolding; should be deleted before commit.
4. **Report columns now misalign**: `report` pads labels to 32 (`{label:<32}` at line 64), but both new ring labels — `"ring_one_msg_1t (SPSC, in-place):"` — are 33 characters, so the ring lines' numbers shift one column right of the pool/global lines. Either widen the pad or shorten the labels.
5. **Minor**: the new `Ring::init(...).unwrap()` at line 72 has no `// OK:` justification comment (AGENTS.md requires one on non-test `unwrap`; the pre-existing demo unwraps also lack them, so this just continues that drift). And the module doc's Part-1 bullet (lines 8–11) still describes the ring demo as strictly "one producer thread to one consumer thread" — now only true of the 2t variant.

None of these block anything functionally — clippy and the build are clean. Want me to apply the fixes (doc comment, panic messages, dead println, label width), or were some of these intentional?

content.text:
Nice — the numbers line up with mine except one interesting divergence: your 2t ring run is 16.7 ns/msg where mine was 69.1, with the 1t number identical (2.7 ns). Same binary, so that's not code — it's thread placement. The 3900X has two CCDs with four separate L3 complexes (CCXs); the demo spawns two scoped threads and lets the scheduler place them. When producer and consumer land on the same CCX the head/tail/slot cache lines bounce through a shared L3 and you get ~17 ns; when they land on different CCDs the transfer goes over the Infinity Fabric and it balloons toward ~70. We think that's the whole story — you'd see it flip run to run, and pinning with `taskset -c 0,1` vs `taskset -c 0,12` would reproduce both ends deterministically if you want to confirm.

The rest of the story the numbers tell:

- **1t ring at 2.7 ns** is the ring's own instruction cost — everything stays in L1, no coherence traffic. That's the honest "primitive overhead" number.
- **pool 2t at 282 ns** is not the pool — it's the `std::sync::mpsc::sync_channel` carrying the guards, plus the same cross-core traffic. The pool's own cost is the 1t line: 9.7 ns per alloc+free.
- **pool 1t (9.7) vs global (7.1)**: Box::new/drop on a hot loop hits malloc's thread-local fast path, which is very good; the pool isn't ahead in this shape. The pool's value is the cross-thread/shared-memory story, not beating thread-local malloc on a single thread.

One thought for the demo's honesty: since 2t placement makes the number swing 4× between runs, it might be worth either noting that in the demo's doc comment, or eventually pinning threads — though per your Ideas note, real benchmarking belongs in iiac-perf with its calibrated harness, so a doc note may be all this demo wants.

What did you want to dig into?

questions.question: "Same core" is ambiguous on the 3900X — pinning both threads to the same logical CPU would just measure the scheduler time-slicing two spin loops. Which pinned variants do you want?

options.label: SMT siblings + cross-CCD (Recommended)
options.description: Two pinned runs: both threads on one physical core's two hyperthreads (shared L1/L2 — the cheapest handoff), and on cores in different CCDs (worst case, over Infinity Fabric). Plus keep the existing unpinned run.

options.label: Add same-CCX too (3 pinned)
options.description: SMT siblings, same-CCX different cores (shared L3), and cross-CCD — the full latency ladder on Zen 2.

options.label: Literally same logical CPU
options.description: Pin both threads to one logical CPU anyway — measures forced context-switching, not cache traffic. Probably not what you want, but it is what "same core" says literally.

questions.question: How should the demo pin threads?

options.label: libc crate (Recommended)
options.description: Add `libc` as a dependency and call sched_setaffinity directly; read /sys/devices/system/cpu/*/topology to discover SMT siblings and L3 groups at runtime, so it works on any Linux box, with a skip-if-unavailable fallback.

options.label: core_affinity crate
options.description: Cross-platform pinning crate, simpler API, but it only enumerates core IDs — no SMT/L3 topology, so sibling/CCD pairs would still need /sys parsing on Linux.

options.label: No new dep — raw /sys + extern
options.description: Hand-declare sched_setaffinity as an unsafe extern "C" fn and parse /sys. Zero deps, Linux-only, a little ugly.

content.text:
Your understanding is right, and it's the core of the experiment: two SMT siblings share their physical core's L1/L2 cache, so the producer→consumer cache-line handoff is nearly free, while two different physical cores must move the lines through L3 or the fabric. (On your 3900X "different physical cores" has a further split — same L3 complex vs. different CCD — which is why your unpinned runs wander between ~17 and ~70 ns. I'll have the "different cores" variant prefer a core outside cpu0's L3 group so it shows the far end deterministically.)

I'll add: `libc` (Linux-only, for `sched_setaffinity`), topology discovery from `/sys` (SMT sibling pair + a far core), and parameterize `ring_one_msg_2t` with an optional pin pair — keeping the unpinned run as-is.

content.text:
Both pinned variants are in and the run shows exactly the effect you were after:

```
ring_one_msg_1t (SPSC, in-place):  363,906,713 msgs/sec      2.7 ns/msg
ring_one_msg_2t (unpinned):         18,923,563 msgs/sec     52.8 ns/msg
ring_one_msg_2t (same core 0+12):  130,695,531 msgs/sec      7.7 ns/msg
ring_one_msg_2t (diff cores 0+3):    5,198,587 msgs/sec    192.4 ns/msg
```

Same physical core (cpu0 + its SMT sibling cpu12, shared L1/L2) runs at 7.7 ns/msg — 25× faster than different physical cores at 192 ns/msg. The unpinned number lands in between, which is the scheduler wandering. Note the "diff cores" pair deliberately picks a core **outside cpu0's L3 group** (on the 3900X, cpu0's L3 covers cores 0–2, so it chose cpu3 in the next CCX) — that's the far end of the ladder; a different-core-same-L3 pair would land somewhere in the middle.

What landed:

- `Cargo.toml`: `libc` as a Linux-only target dependency for `sched_setaffinity`.
- `discover_pin_pairs()` reads `/sys/devices/system/cpu` topology at runtime — SMT sibling pair from `thread_siblings_list`, far pair preferring a cpu outside cpu0's `cache/index3/shared_cpu_list` — so it adapts to any Linux box and each pinned run prints a "skipped" line instead when the machine lacks the shape (single-core, SMT off).
- `ring_one_msg_2t(pin: PinPair)` — each thread pins itself when a pair is given; `None` keeps the original unpinned behavior. `pin_to_cpu` panics on failure so a mislabeled number can't slip through.
- Non-Linux stubs so the demo still compiles elsewhere (pinned runs just skip).
- fmt, clippy, and tests are all clean (a `PinPair` type alias resolved a `type_complexity` warning).

One caveat worth stating: 192 ns for cross-L3 is notably worse than your earlier ~70 ns unpinned worst case. We think the fixed 0+3 pairing plus both threads spinning hard on the same lines produces a steadier, fuller ping-pong than scheduler-placed threads that occasionally migrate — but that's inference, not measured. Worth watching across a few runs on your side.

content.text:
Agree with the direction — with the pinned variants, the parenthetical is doing run-condition work ("unpinned", "same core 0+12"), so the queue discipline belongs in the name, not the label. `spsc_ring_one_msg_1t` also sets up a clean contrast for later variants (the pool's free path is already MPSC-shaped, and descriptor queues will reuse the SPSC ring — names like `spsc_ring_desc_...` extend naturally). And dropping "(SPSC, in-place)" from `_1t` is right once the name carries it: `_2t` already dropped it for the pin condition, so `_1t` keeping it was asymmetric anyway.

One consequence to decide before I apply it: label width. The current pad is 33; the new longest label — `spsc_ring_one_msg_2t (same core 0+12):` — is 39 characters, which puts the full line at ~79 columns. Two ways to go:

1. **Widen the pad to 39** and keep labels verbatim (`spsc_ring_one_msg_2t (same core 0+12):`). Simple, but the lines nearly fill 80 columns and a 2-digit cpu pair (e.g. `10+22` on another box) would push past.
2. **Shorten the parentheticals** — `(smt 0+12)` / `(far 0+3)` / `(unpinned)` — keeping the pad around 33–34 with headroom.

I'd lean to option 1 for readability ("same core" / "diff cores" say what "smt"/"far" make the reader decode), accepting the wide lines. Want me to go ahead with that, or do you prefer the terse labels?

content.text:
Essentially yes. `pool_demo` is: alloc a pool buffer on thread A, move its `BufSlot` guard across a `std::sync::mpsc::sync_channel`, assert and free on thread B — so in the naming scheme we just established, the transport is std's mpsc channel, the cargo is one pool message, two threads: `std_mpsc_one_pool_msg_2t` describes it accurately.

Two nuances worth noting before you commit to the name:

- **It's std's mpsc used SPSC.** The channel type is multi-producer, but the demo has exactly one sender and one receiver — the same discipline as the ring runs. So the name is honest about the *mechanism* (which is what determines the cost — std's channel pays for its MPSC generality either way), but the *usage* is SPSC. That's actually the interesting comparison: SPSC ring at ~7–200 ns depending on placement vs. std's channel at ~280 ns doing the same one-producer-one-consumer job.
- **It measures pool + channel together.** The line is "alloc here → free there," so ~280 ns is channel transport plus the pool's cross-thread free path, not the channel alone. The pool's own cost is isolated by `pool_alloc_free_demo` (9.6 ns).

If the goal is renaming for consistency, the set would land as something like `std_mpsc_one_pool_msg_2t` (pool_demo), `pool_alloc_free_1t` (pool_alloc_free_demo), `global_alloc_free_1t` (global_alloc_free_demo) — with labels shrinking the same way the ring ones did. Want me to do that?

`std_mpsc_one_pool_msg_2t` now takes the same `PinPair` parameter as the ring 2t (allocator thread gets the first cpu, freer the second) and `main` reuses the pairs from `discover_pin_pairs`, so both 2t families run the identical unpinned / same-core / diff-cores ladder. The pad widened to 43 for the new longest label, and the skipped-fallback lines were re-padded to match.

The comparison now reads nicely: at every placement the ring beats std's channel + pool by roughly 2–5× (same core 11.4 vs 62.8, diff cores 185.6 vs 308.3), and the gap narrows as cache-line transfer cost dominates both — which is what you'd expect once the interconnect, not the queue discipline, is the bottleneck.

325	## Messaging layer: pools and descriptor queues
326	
327	Design for the layer above the ring. The pool half is
328	as-built as of the 0.6.0 cycle (`src/pool.rs`: layout,
329	init/attach, alloc/free); descriptor queues and provenance
330	remain design. The sections above are the as-built record of
331	the ring itself.
332	
333	The ring's `reserve_slot` fuses three acts that a messaging
334	system needs separated: it allocates message memory (the slot),
335	assigns queue position (the reservation is the ring head), and
336	opens a publication window (the guard exclusively borrows the
337	endpoint, blocking all other sends). Getting a message must not
338	imply sending it now — or ever. The fix is a layer, not a ring
339	rework: pools own message memory, queues carry small
340	descriptors, and the existing ring is the unchanged primitive
341	underneath (reserve-to-commit becomes a momentary act, where
342	the fusion is harmless).
343	
344	### Layer requirements
345	
346	- **Decoupled get/hold/send/free** — allocate a message, hold
347	  it indefinitely, send it over any queue later, forward it,
348	  or free it unsent.
349	- **Any quantity of queues and pools** in a system, and any
350	  message can travel over any queue.
351	- **Inter- and intra-process** — processes share queue ends,
352	  and message payloads zero-copy across process boundaries.
353	  The embedded single-address-space case is the degenerate
354	  mapping where all peers share one base.
355	- **Heterogeneous pools** — pools differ arbitrarily in buffer
356	  size and count; each meets the alignment constraints and
357	  self-describes its geometry.
358	- **Phasing** — queues are SPSC rings now, an MPSC ring is a
359	  future sibling primitive; pools have a single allocator
360	  now, shared allocation eventually.
361	
362	### Provenance and descriptors
363	
364	- **Offsets only, everywhere** — different per-process
365	  mappings mean no pointers in any shared structure; this
366	  also serves the single-address-space case unchanged.
367	- **Message provenance** — every buffer begins with a small
368	  header naming its pool `(pool id, offset)`; whoever holds a
369	  message last must free it to the right pool, so provenance
370	  travels with the message.
371	- **Descriptors** — a queue entry is a uniform small
372	  `(pool id, buffer offset)` regardless of message size; the
373	  receiver resolves the pool id and learns geometry from the
374	  pool's own header.
375	- **Pool self-description** — each pool region starts with a
376	  header (magic, layout version, buffer size, count,
377	  alignment), attach-validated exactly like the ring header.
378	- **Pool-id resolution** — each process keeps a local registry
379	  mapping pool id to its own mapping of that pool's region.
380	  "Any message over any queue" carries the precondition that
381	  both endpoints have the message's pool mapped.
382	- **Trust** — offsets and indices arriving through shared
383	  memory are untrusted: bounds- and alignment-checked against
384	  the receiver's own view before use, same discipline as the
385	  ring's geometry snapshot.

file.content:
## Messaging layer: pools and descriptor queues

Design for the layer above the ring. The pool half is
as-built as of the 0.6.0 cycle (`src/pool.rs`: layout,
init/attach, alloc/free); descriptor queues and provenance
remain design. The sections above are the as-built record of
the ring itself.

The ring's `reserve_slot` fuses three acts that a messaging
system needs separated: it allocates message memory (the slot),
assigns queue position (the reservation is the ring head), and
opens a publication window (the guard exclusively borrows the
endpoint, blocking all other sends). Getting a message must not
imply sending it now — or ever. The fix is a layer, not a ring
rework: pools own message memory, queues carry small
descriptors, and the existing ring is the unchanged primitive
underneath (reserve-to-commit becomes a momentary act, where
the fusion is harmless).

### Layer requirements

- **Decoupled get/hold/send/free** — allocate a message, hold
  it indefinitely, send it over any queue later, forward it,
  or free it unsent.
- **Any quantity of queues and pools** in a system, and any
  message can travel over any queue.
- **Inter- and intra-process** — processes share queue ends,
  and message payloads zero-copy across process boundaries.
  The embedded single-address-space case is the degenerate
  mapping where all peers share one base.
- **Heterogeneous pools** — pools differ arbitrarily in buffer
  size and count; each meets the alignment constraints and
  self-describes its geometry.
- **Phasing** — queues are SPSC rings now, an MPSC ring is a
  future sibling primitive; pools have a single allocator
  now, shared allocation eventually.

### Provenance and descriptors

- **Offsets only, everywhere** — different per-process
  mappings mean no pointers in any shared structure; this
  also serves the single-address-space case unchanged.
- **Message provenance** — every buffer begins with a small
  header naming its pool `(pool id, offset)`; whoever holds a
  message last must free it to the right pool, so provenance
  travels with the message.
- **Descriptors** — a queue entry is a uniform small
  `(pool id, buffer offset)` regardless of message size; the
  receiver resolves the pool id and learns geometry from the
  pool's own header.
- **Pool self-description** — each pool region starts with a
  header (magic, layout version, buffer size, count,
  alignment), attach-validated exactly like the ring header.
- **Pool-id resolution** — each process keeps a local registry
  mapping pool id to its own mapping of that pool's region.
  "Any message over any queue" carries the precondition that
  both endpoints have the message's pool mapped.
- **Trust** — offsets and indices arriving through shared
  memory are untrusted: bounds- and alignment-checked against
  the receiver's own view before use, same discipline as the
  ring's geometry snapshot.

content.text:
Good news and bad news. I checked the pool API (`src/pool.rs`) — the answer is: **not possible with the current public API, but it's two small methods away**, and those two methods are a deliberately scoped slice of Todo #1 (descriptor queues).

What the composed flow needs, and what exists:

- **A second pool handle on the consumer thread** — ✅ exists. `Pool::attach` (`src/pool.rs:174`) gives another handle over the same region, and the contract already allows it: one handle allocates, any handle frees.
- **Getting a descriptor out of a `BufSlot`** — ❌ missing. The buffer index (`idx`, `src/pool.rs:321`) is private, and the guard's only exits are `free()` or leak. There's no `into_idx(self) -> u32` that says "I'm converting my ownership into a descriptor."
- **Rebuilding a `BufSlot` from a descriptor** — ❌ missing. The attached consumer handle has no `from_idx(idx) -> BufSlot` — no way to mint a guard (or even a `&T`) from an index it received.

So the demo shape would be: producer `pool.alloc::<Msg>()` → write `seq` → `slot.into_idx()` → send the `u32` through the SPSC ring as the message; consumer reads the `u32` from the ring → `pool.from_idx::<Msg>(idx)` → assert → `free()`. The ring's commit/reserve Release/Acquire already transfers visibility of the payload writes, so the ordering story is sound; `from_idx` bounds-checks the index and is `unsafe` only because ownership transfer ("this idx came from `into_idx` and wasn't duplicated") can't be proven by types — exactly the trust posture the design doc already prescribes for descriptors arriving through shared memory.

Without those methods there's no honest version: you could FIFO the guards through a `VecDeque` (1t) or a std channel (2t) alongside the ring, but then the side channel is doing the real ownership transport and the ring is decoration.

Note this is the intentionally-small version of Todo #1: full descriptor queues carry `(pool id, buffer offset)` with a per-process pool registry and provenance headers, so any message travels over any queue. The demo needs none of that — one pool, one ring, bare `u32` index — and building the small slice first would validate the mechanics the full design rests on.

Since this means library changes, it's a new cycle. Want me to open one — roughly: `-1` add `BufSlot::into_idx` + `Pool::from_idx` with protocol tests, `-2` the two demo functions (`spsc_ring_one_pool_msg_1t/2t` with the same pin ladder), close-out — or would you rather go straight at the full Todo #1 design instead?

content.content:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
4	contains instructions on how a commit cycle is accomplished.
5	
6	The artifact a cycle produces is whatever the bot generates from
7	the conversation — code, prose, an image, a song, a screenplay.
8	The steps below use a Rust crate as the running example (the
9	cargo cycle, `Cargo.toml` versioning); substitute your medium's
10	equivalents — this project's manifest is recorded in
11	[versioning.md](versioning.md).
12	
13	## Cycles
14	
15	A cycle has three phases:
16	
17	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
18	  first commit, when it needs setup (a lightweight cycle omits
19	  it and starts at `-1` — see
20	  [versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)). Sets up the
21	  cycle:
22	  - Bump the version-of-record to `X.Y.Z-0` (where it lives
23	    and the suffix scheme are project-specific — see
24	    [versioning.md](versioning.md)).
25	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
26	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
27	    statement + plan ladder).
28	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
29	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
30	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
31	  needs; each runs through the
32	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
33	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
34	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
35	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
36	    `## Done`.
37	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
38	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
39	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
40	    changed.
41	
42	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
43	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
44	result must always be published at close-out. See
45	[Pushing](#pushing).
46	
47	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
48	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
49	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
50	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
51	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
52	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
53	for the local-ladder mechanics.
54	
55	## Chores sections
56	
57	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
58	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording landed work. In
59	general, every commit that lands on the permanent branch
60	should have a reference to it on a `Commits:` list in a
61	chores file.
62	
63	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
64	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
65	with the title it records (e.g. `## refactor: foo bar`),
66	followed by an **empty `Commits:`** line. The narrative
67	body stays empty until close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out));
68	the `Commits:` line is backfilled later, once the commit is
69	permanent (see [Commits backfill](#commits-backfill) below).
70	
71	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
72	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
73	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
74	
75	### Commits backfill
76	
77	A chores section's `Commits:` line cites the commit(s) it
78	records, by SHA — but a SHA isn't stable until the commit
79	lands on a **permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived
80	release/patch branch that won't be rewritten); a rebase or
81	squash rewrites it on the way. So:
82	
83	- A chores section is **opened with an empty `Commits:`** line.
84	- **Backfill once the commit is on a permanent branch**,
85	  where its SHA is final. A commit can't record its own SHA
86	  (that would change the hash), so the fill always lands one
87	  push later: **each push backfills the `Commits:` of the
88	  commits the previous push made permanent.** On a topic
89	  branch the sections instead wait until the branch lands —
90	  so no SHA is ever written that a later rebase could
91	  invalidate.
92	
93	Use `[[N]]` refs — several as `[[N]],[[M]]` only when one
94	section records multiple commits (a merge non-ff close-out) —
95	with the commit URL + 40-hex SHA in the file's `# References`
96	(format in AGENTS.md
97	[Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references)).
98	A section's `##` title matches its commit title, so a rare
99	deliberate rewrite of a permanent-branch commit re-syncs via
100	`git log --grep "<title>"`.
101	
102	The per-push cadence is a project choice, not dogma — a
103	**per-close-out** model (recording a cycle's SHAs at its
104	close-out) is equally valid. The one invariant: a recorded
105	SHA must be permanent.
106	
107	## Preparation
108	
109	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`), when the cycle needs
110	setup (a lightweight cycle omits it — see
111	[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)):
112	
113	- **Bump the version-of-record** to `X.Y.Z-0`. Where it
114	  lives, the suffix scheme, and any derived files (a
115	  lockfile, a sourced manifest version) are
116	  project-specific — see [versioning.md](versioning.md).
117	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
118	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
119	  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
120	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
121	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
122	  - A **plan ladder**.
123	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
124	  append a `##` header with the title it records — the
125	  cycle's anticipated close-out title.
126	
127	## Work-N
128	
129	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
130	implement the change. As many as needed:
131	
132	- Each commit runs through the
133	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
134	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
135	  visibility).
136	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
137	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
138	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
139	  scope grows enough (see
140	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
141	
142	## Close-out
143	
144	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
145	only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
146	what happens post-squash:
147	
148	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
149	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
150	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
151	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
152	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
153	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
154	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
155	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
156	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
157	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
158	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
159	    back-reference.
160	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
161	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
162	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
163	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
164	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
165	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
166	
167	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
168	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
169	see [Pushing](#pushing).
170	
171	## Numbering
172	
173	Each commit's phase is encoded in the version suffix — `-0`
174	Preparation, `-1`/`-2`/… Work, bare `X.Y.Z` Close-out,
175	recursively for sub-cycles. The full scheme — disambiguation,
176	nesting, optional Preparation, the project's version-of-record
177	format, and the per-phase bump — lives in
178	[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering), which is the
179	single source of truth for this repo's versioning.
180	
181	## Per-commit flow
182	
183	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
184	through:
185	
186	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
187	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
188	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
189	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
190	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
191	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
192	4. **Validate the artifact** — a medium-specific step, skip-able
193	   for notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. For the Rust
194	   example the cargo cycle is:
195	   1. `cargo fmt`
196	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
197	   3. `cargo test`
198	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
199	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
200	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
201	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
202	   changes and we iterate until complete.
203	6. **Write the commit description** — see
204	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
205	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
206	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
207	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
208	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
209	   keeps the verb visible):
210	
211	   ```
212	   jj commit -m \
213	   "<type>: <short description>" \
214	   -m "<intro paragraph>
215	
216	   - file1: gist
217	   - file2: gist
218	
219	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
220	   -R .
221	   ```
222	
223	**Two overrides apply:**
224	
225	- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
226	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
227	  surface it; don't push through.
228	- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
229	  a review or question forward.
230	
231	## Commit description
232	
233	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/):
234	
235	```
236	<type>: <short description>
237	```
238	
239	Titles carry **no trailing `(<version>)` suffix**. The
240	version-of-record — where it lives and its bump cadence, see
241	[versioning.md](versioning.md) — is useful for confirming
242	you're running the version you're testing, not a per-commit
243	title marker. We think per-commit version-in-title is
244	unstable on large projects: many changes are in flight
245	simultaneously, and a
246	version is only stable once merged into the main repo — so
247	titles don't carry one.
248	
249	### Title
250	
251	- ≤50 chars total.
252	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
253	  `docs`, `chore`.
254	- Favor terse phrasings.
255	- **Distinct per step** — each of a cycle's commits gets its
256	  own descriptive title (no shared cycle title with a step
257	  marker). Share a greppable stem across the cycle's titles
258	  (e.g. `ring buffer`) so `git log --grep` collects them; the
259	  chores section header matches the close-out title. See
260	  AGENTS.md
261	  [Conventional-commit shape](../AGENTS.md#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit).
262	
263	### Body
264	
265	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
266	wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:
267	
268	- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
269	  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
270	  files with multiple distinct changes:
271	
272	  ```
273	  - path/to/file1
274	    - first distinct change
275	    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
276	       with continuation indented 5
277	  ```
278	
279	  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
280	  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
281	  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
282	  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.
283	
284	- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
285	  in-session activity rather than code changes.
286	
287	### Trailer
288	
289	`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
290	[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
291	in AGENTS.md for the convention.
292	
293	For breaking changes, use the hyphenated `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
294	trailer key. `BREAKING CHANGE:` (with a space) is the only
295	space-separated key the Conventional Commits spec allows; the
296	hyphenated form is also valid and avoids the space ambiguity.
297	
298	## Reviewing changes
299	
300	Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
301	on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
302	editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:
303	
304	- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
305	- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
306	- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
307	- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev
308	
309	Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
310	it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
311	`jj show -r @-`.
312	
313	See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
314	close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
315	are in [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets).
316	
317	## Pushing
318	
319	### Policy
320	
321	Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
322	progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
323	the cycle's result must be published.
324	
325	**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
326	cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
327	happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
328	published and explicitly approved that specific push.
329	Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
330	the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.
331	
332	**Default is interactive; an explicit scoped delegation waives
333	the gates.** The gates above — per-push approval, the
334	commit-description review (show title+body and stop), and the
335	hard stop after push/finalize — are the *interactive default*.
336	They yield when the user **explicitly** delegates a complete,
337	bounded task and authorizes carrying it through ("do all of X
338	and push each step, don't check in"). The bot then proceeds
339	through that task's commits and pushes without stopping, and
340	continues past each push to the next step. Conditions:
341	
342	- **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
343	  well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
344	  completion. "Commit and push" (or "then push") names the
345	  destination, not a waiver: it authorizes the push *after*
346	  the normal work review and description review, not skipping
347	  them. Only wording that explicitly waives the stops ("don't
348	  check in", "no need to review", "carry it through
349	  unattended") waives them.
350	- **Bounded goal** — covers the named task only; does not carry
351	  to the next task or a vaguer follow-on.
352	- **Destructive ops still pause** — delegation covers the task's
353	  ordinary commits and pushes; it does *not* pre-authorize a
354	  genuinely irreversible action (force-push over published
355	  history, history rewrite, deleting a remote branch). Those can
356	  permanently destroy work and aren't a normal cycle step, so the
357	  bot flags one before acting. An ordinary delegated cycle never
358	  reaches this.
359	- **Still transparent** — report each commit/push as it lands
360	  (title + outcome) so the user can catch up.
361	- **When in doubt, ask** — ambiguous authorization falls back to
362	  per-push approval.
363	
364	### Shape at close-out push
365	
366	At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
367	shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
368	the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
369	the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
370	needs approval), so choose deliberately.
371	
372	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
373	  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
374	  focused on one or two files.
375	- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
376	  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
377	  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
378	  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
379	  for the full setup sequence.
380	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
381	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
382	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
383	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
384	
385	Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
386	`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
387	
388	### Merge non-ff recipe
389	
390	Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
391	close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
392	close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
393	(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
394	Work commit:
395	
396	1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
397	   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
398	   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
399	   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
400	   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.
401	
402	   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
403	   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
404	2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
405	   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
406	   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
407	   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
408	3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.
409	
410	**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
411	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
412	immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
413	push force-updates `main`.
414	The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
415	the close-out push.
416	
417	### vc-x1 push wrapper
418	
419	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
420	`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.
421	
422	**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
423	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
424	shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
425	planned:
426	
427	- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
428	- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
429	- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).
430	
431	### .claude cadence
432	
433	**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
434	with every code commit in that push.
435	
436	The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
437	across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
438	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
439	code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.
440	
441	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
442	on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
443	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
444	app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
445	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
446	
447	Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
448	session information created, which we don't worry about.
449	The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
450	remain empty.
451	
452	### Bot communication at the reviews
453	
454	Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
455	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
456	
457	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
458	  "Work complete. Please review."
459	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
460	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
461	  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
462	  --body ...` invocation by default.
463	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
464	  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
465	  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
466	  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.
467	
468	### After push or finalize: stop and wait
469	
470	After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
471	**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
472	.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
473	hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
474	proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
475	user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
476	step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
477	stop for the whole turn.
478	
479	`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
480	then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
481	no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
482	closing words *before* invoking it. The harness rejects an empty
483	turn, so it may force a visible token after the tool returns; if
484	so, emit a bare acknowledgment only (e.g. "landed") — never a
485	summary, verification, or next-step offer.
486	
487	Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
488	squashes → pushes):
489	
490	- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
491	  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
492	  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
493	  the invoke — into the snapshot.
494	- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
495	  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
496	  the bot to do more work.
497	- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
498	  after the tool returns. The harness may force a visible token
499	  (it rejects an empty turn) — keep it to a bare acknowledgment,
500	  never a summary or a next step.
501	
502	**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
503	finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
504	saying, say it *before* the invoke.
505	
506	### Recovery
507	
508	- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
509	  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
510	  finalize by hand:
511	
512	  ```
513	  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
514	  ```
515	
516	- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
517	  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
518	  extra activity by the bot's agents).
519	  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
520	    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
521	    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
522	    *after* the snapshot.
523	  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
524	    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
525	    change; it is not under the user's control.)
526	  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
527	    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
528	    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
529	  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
530	    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
531	    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
532	    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
533	    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
534	    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
535	- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
536	  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
537	  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
538	  stale stage.
539	- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
540	  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
541	  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
542	  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
543	  push.
544	
545	## Iterative work
546	
547	When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
548	from incremental review, loop:
549	
550	1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
551	2. Make the next round of changes.
552	3. User reviews the round (see
553	   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
554	4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
555	   new empty `@`.
556	5. If not done, go to step 2.
557	
558	Same jj mechanics as a
559	[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
560	single-commit scope — the version
561	doesn't change.
562	
563	## Sub-cycle ladders
564	
565	When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
566	[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
567	commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
568	**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
569	parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
570	for review and bisection only.
571	
572	### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder
573	
574	For each Work commit in the ladder:
575	
576	1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
577	2. Do the commit's work.
578	3. Run the fast validation (Rust example: `cargo test
579	   --bins`). **Non-negotiable** — for code, build and clippy
580	   alone miss regressions until a later commit runs the full
581	   suite, raising bisection cost.
582	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
583	   only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
584	   into one final commit.
585	
586	### Navigating the ladder
587	
588	Common moves:
589	
590	- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
591	  from its base.
592	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
593	  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
594	- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
595	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
596	  isolation.
597	
598	Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
599	descendants auto-rebase.
600	
601	### Close-out: squash the ladder
602	
603	When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:
604	
605	```
606	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
607	```
608	
609	`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
610	keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
611	After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
612	intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.
613	
614	Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.
615	
616	For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
617	when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.
618	
619	### Recovery
620	
621	If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
622	prior commits:
623	
624	- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
625	  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
626	  parent.
627	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
628	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
629	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
630	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
631	  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
632	  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
633	  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.
634	
635	# References
636	
637	- [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets) — revset primitives
638	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
639	- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
640	  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
641	  for squash-recipe experiments.
642	- The per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate exists because a
643	  regression introduced in an early ladder commit can go
644	  uncaught until a later commit runs the full suite, raising
645	  bisection cost.
646

# Cycle protocol

This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains instructions on how a commit cycle is accomplished.

The artifact a cycle produces is whatever the bot generates from
the conversation — code, prose, an image, a song, a screenplay.
The steps below use a Rust crate as the running example (the
cargo cycle, `Cargo.toml` versioning); substitute your medium's
equivalents — this project's manifest is recorded in
[versioning.md](versioning.md).

## Cycles

A cycle has three phases:

- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
  first commit, when it needs setup (a lightweight cycle omits
  it and starts at `-1` — see
  [versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)). Sets up the
  cycle:
  - Bump the version-of-record to `X.Y.Z-0` (where it lives
    and the suffix scheme are project-specific — see
    [versioning.md](versioning.md)).
  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
    statement + plan ladder).
  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
  needs; each runs through the
  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
    `## Done`.
  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
    [chores section](#chores-sections).
  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
    changed.

A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
result must always be published at close-out. See
[Pushing](#pushing).

**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
for the local-ladder mechanics.

## Chores sections

A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording landed work. In
general, every commit that lands on the permanent branch
should have a reference to it on a `Commits:` list in a
chores file.

The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the title it records (e.g. `## refactor: foo bar`),
followed by an **empty `Commits:`** line. The narrative
body stays empty until close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out));
the `Commits:` line is backfilled later, once the commit is
permanent (see [Commits backfill](#commits-backfill) below).

Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).

### Commits backfill

A chores section's `Commits:` line cites the commit(s) it
records, by SHA — but a SHA isn't stable until the commit
lands on a **permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived
release/patch branch that won't be rewritten); a rebase or
squash rewrites it on the way. So:

- A chores section is **opened with an empty `Commits:`** line.
- **Backfill once the commit is on a permanent branch**,
  where its SHA is final. A commit can't record its own SHA
  (that would change the hash), so the fill always lands one
  push later: **each push backfills the `Commits:` of the
  commits the previous push made permanent.** On a topic
  branch the sections instead wait until the branch lands —
  so no SHA is ever written that a later rebase could
  invalidate.

Use `[[N]]` refs — several as `[[N]],[[M]]` only when one
section records multiple commits (a merge non-ff close-out) —
with the commit URL + 40-hex SHA in the file's `# References`
(format in AGENTS.md
[Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references)).
A section's `##` title matches its commit title, so a rare
deliberate rewrite of a permanent-branch commit re-syncs via
`git log --grep "<title>"`.

The per-push cadence is a project choice, not dogma — a
**per-close-out** model (recording a cycle's SHAs at its
close-out) is equally valid. The one invariant: a recorded
SHA must be permanent.

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`), when the cycle needs
setup (a lightweight cycle omits it — see
[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)):

- **Bump the version-of-record** to `X.Y.Z-0`. Where it
  lives, the suffix scheme, and any derived files (a
  lockfile, a sourced manifest version) are
  project-specific — see [versioning.md](versioning.md).
- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
  - A **plan ladder**.
- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
  append a `##` header with the title it records — the
  cycle's anticipated close-out title.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
implement the change. As many as needed:

- Each commit runs through the
  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
  visibility).
- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
  [Pushing](#pushing)).
- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
  scope grows enough (see
  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
what happens post-squash:

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
  placeholder opened during Preparation):
  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
    chores `##` header already carries that string).
  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
    back-reference.
  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
  - Replace `## In Progress` with
    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Numbering

Each commit's phase is encoded in the version suffix — `-0`
Preparation, `-1`/`-2`/… Work, bare `X.Y.Z` Close-out,
recursively for sub-cycles. The full scheme — disambiguation,
nesting, optional Preparation, the project's version-of-record
format, and the per-phase bump — lives in
[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering), which is the
single source of truth for this repo's versioning.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
   for the loop-and-squash technique).
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
4. **Validate the artifact** — a medium-specific step, skip-able
   for notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. For the Rust
   example the cargo cycle is:
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   changes and we iterate until complete.
6. **Write the commit description** — see
   [Commit description](#commit-description).
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description>" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

## Commit description

[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/):

```
<type>: <short description>
```

Titles carry **no trailing `(<version>)` suffix**. The
version-of-record — where it lives and its bump cadence, see
[versioning.md](versioning.md) — is useful for confirming
you're running the version you're testing, not a per-commit
title marker. We think per-commit version-in-title is
unstable on large projects: many changes are in flight
simultaneously, and a
version is only stable once merged into the main repo — so
titles don't carry one.

### Title

- ≤50 chars total.
- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
  `docs`, `chore`.
- Favor terse phrasings.
- **Distinct per step** — each of a cycle's commits gets its
  own descriptive title (no shared cycle title with a step
  marker). Share a greppable stem across the cycle's titles
  (e.g. `ring buffer`) so `git log --grep` collects them; the
  chores section header matches the close-out title. See
  AGENTS.md
  [Conventional-commit shape](../AGENTS.md#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit).

### Body

[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:

- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
  files with multiple distinct changes:

  ```
  - path/to/file1
    - first distinct change
    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
       with continuation indented 5
  ```

  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.

- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
  in-session activity rather than code changes.

### Trailer

`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
in AGENTS.md for the convention.

For breaking changes, use the hyphenated `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
trailer key. `BREAKING CHANGE:` (with a space) is the only
space-separated key the Conventional Commits spec allows; the
hyphenated form is also valid and avoids the space ambiguity.

## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:

- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev

Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
are in [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets).

## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
the cycle's result must be published.

**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
published and explicitly approved that specific push.
Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.

**Default is interactive; an explicit scoped delegation waives
the gates.** The gates above — per-push approval, the
commit-description review (show title+body and stop), and the
hard stop after push/finalize — are the *interactive default*.
They yield when the user **explicitly** delegates a complete,
bounded task and authorizes carrying it through ("do all of X
and push each step, don't check in"). The bot then proceeds
through that task's commits and pushes without stopping, and
continues past each push to the next step. Conditions:

- **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
  well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
  completion. "Commit and push" (or "then push") names the
  destination, not a waiver: it authorizes the push *after*
  the normal work review and description review, not skipping
  them. Only wording that explicitly waives the stops ("don't
  check in", "no need to review", "carry it through
  unattended") waives them.
- **Bounded goal** — covers the named task only; does not carry
  to the next task or a vaguer follow-on.
- **Destructive ops still pause** — delegation covers the task's
  ordinary commits and pushes; it does *not* pre-authorize a
  genuinely irreversible action (force-push over published
  history, history rewrite, deleting a remote branch). Those can
  permanently destroy work and aren't a normal cycle step, so the
  bot flags one before acting. An ordinary delegated cycle never
  reaches this.
- **Still transparent** — report each commit/push as it lands
  (title + outcome) so the user can catch up.
- **When in doubt, ask** — ambiguous authorization falls back to
  per-push approval.

### Shape at close-out push

At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
needs approval), so choose deliberately.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
  for the full setup sequence.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### Merge non-ff recipe

Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.

   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
push force-updates `main`.
The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
the close-out push.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.

**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
planned:

- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).

### .claude cadence

**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
with every code commit in that push.

The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
session information created, which we don't worry about.
The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
remain empty.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
  --body ...` invocation by default.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.

### After push or finalize: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
stop for the whole turn.

`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
closing words *before* invoking it. The harness rejects an empty
turn, so it may force a visible token after the tool returns; if
so, emit a bare acknowledgment only (e.g. "landed") — never a
summary, verification, or next-step offer.

Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
squashes → pushes):

- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
  the invoke — into the snapshot.
- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
  the bot to do more work.
- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
  after the tool returns. The harness may force a visible token
  (it rejects an empty turn) — keep it to a bare acknowledgment,
  never a summary or a next step.

**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
saying, say it *before* the invoke.

### Recovery

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
  finalize by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
  ```

- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
  extra activity by the bot's agents).
  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
    *after* the snapshot.
  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
    change; it is not under the user's control.)
  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
  stale stage.
- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
  push.

## Iterative work

When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
from incremental review, loop:

1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
2. Make the next round of changes.
3. User reviews the round (see
   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
   new empty `@`.
5. If not done, go to step 2.

Same jj mechanics as a
[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
single-commit scope — the version
doesn't change.

## Sub-cycle ladders

When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
for review and bisection only.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work.
3. Run the fast validation (Rust example: `cargo test
   --bins`). **Non-negotiable** — for code, build and clippy
   alone miss regressions until a later commit runs the full
   suite, raising bisection cost.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
   only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
   into one final commit.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.

For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.

# References

- [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- The per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate exists because a
  regression introduced in an early ladder commit can go
  uncaught until a later commit runs the full suite, raising
  bisection cost.

1	# Versioning
2	
3	How this project versions its commits and the running
4	artifact. The scheme — the cycle step-numbering and the
5	unique-per-commit aim — is generic and shared across projects:
6	this file is copied **verbatim**, with
7	[Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
8	covering each medium by conditional rather than per-project
9	edits.
10	
11	## Terms
12	
13	Three names, used as defined here across
14	[AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md),
15	[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md), and the notes files:
16	
17	- **version** — the per-commit version (e.g. `0.3.0-5.3.0`),
18	  written with `-` in prose, ladders, chores, todo, and commit
19	  talk; its suffix encodes the cycle phase (see
20	  [Step numbering](#step-numbering)).
21	- **version-of-record** — the authoritative stored copy of the
22	  version, in the project's manifest (see
23	  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record));
24	  a built or running artifact derives from it.
25	- **versioning** — the topic: this scheme as a whole.
26	
27	## Recording the version-of-record
28	
29	Where the version-of-record lives, how it's stored and
30	surfaced, and how often it changes — pick the case that fits
31	your medium:
32	
33	- **Manifest** — where the version-of-record is stored:
34	  - if Rust, `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
35	  - if Python, `pyproject.toml` `[project].version` (or the
36	    committed config it's sourced from)
37	  - otherwise wherever the medium records it (a generic
38	    `version.toml`, a book's frontmatter, …) — add the case as
39	    needed
40	- **Notation** — how the `-` form is stored:
41	  - if the format allows `-` (TOML `version.toml`, `Cargo.toml`),
42	    store it verbatim
43	  - if it bars `-` (PEP 440's local segment, e.g. a Python
44	    project), remap to `+` — `0.3.0-5.3.0` → `0.3.0+5.3.0`: same
45	    version, just the stored spelling
46	- **Reporter** — how a built artifact surfaces the
47	  version-of-record:
48	  - if a CLI app, `<cli-app> -V`
49	  - if a TUI/GUI, add to Help/About or display on the title
50	- **Cadence** — how often to bump: see
51	  [Unique per commit](#unique-per-commit-preference-not-requirement);
52	  this project follows the per-commit preference.
53	
54	## Unique per commit (preference, not requirement)
55	
56	Our general notion is that the version-of-record should change
57	on **every commit**, so a built or running artifact identifies
58	the exact commit it came from.
59	
60	- This is a preference, **not** a hard requirement. A project
61	  following the cycle protocol may bump less often — once per
62	  cycle, only at release, and so on. Record the choice in
63	  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
64	  if it differs.
65	- It is achievable because the cycle's versions (below) are
66	  **pre-assignable**, unlike the git SHA, which a commit cannot
67	  contain (see the cycle protocol's
68	  [Commits backfill](cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill)).
69	
70	## Step numbering
71	
72	The cycle (Preparation -> Work -> Close-out; see
73	[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md)) encodes each commit's
74	phase in the version suffix — the **final identifier `0` marks
75	a Preparation**:
76	
77	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
78	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
79	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
80	
81	**Preparation is optional.** A lightweight cycle — no ladder,
82	no setup commit — skips `-0` and starts at `-1` (its first
83	Work commit). The same holds at every level: a sub-cycle
84	needing no Preparation omits its `.0` (see Nesting). One that
85	grows a Preparation later adds the `0` step without
86	renumbering siblings.
87	
88	Disambiguation:
89	
90	- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
91	  Preparation.
92	- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
93	  identifier `0`).
94	
95	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
96	
97	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
98	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
99	- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
100	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
101	
102	Bump the version-of-record at the start of each phase, so the
103	active phase is recorded — and, per the preference above,
104	every commit carries a distinct version.
